concepts.title: Area along X axis
concepts.conceptBody:
Area of Bounded Regions

1. For a continuous function $f(x)$ defined over $[a, b]$, the area bounded by the curve $y=f(x)$, the $x$-axis and the ordinates $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a}$ and $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{b}$ is given by $ \int_a^b f(x) d x \quad \int_a^b y d x $

Other types

2. When $f(x)$ is below $x$-axis then the value of integral will come out to be negative but as we know area is a positive quantity. $ \therefore \text { Area }=-\int_a^b f(x) d x=\left|\int_a^b f(x) d x\right| $

  

3. Area of such curves is obtained by using the above 2 types \begin{aligned} \text { Area } & =\text { Area (I) }+ \text { Area (II) }+ \text { Area (III) } \\ & =\int_a^b f(x) d x-\int_b^c f(x) d x+\int_c^d f(x) d x \end{aligned}

concepts.title: Area along Y axis
concepts.conceptBody:
Region of Curve Along the Axis

Sometimes it is easier to take area with $\mathrm{y}$-axis as in following cases: 

1. Area $=\int_a^b f(y) d y$ Note: The function inside the integral is a function of $y$ not $x$. It is because we have to integrate with respect to $y$.

 

 

2. $\begin{aligned} \text { Area } & =-\int_a^b f(y) d y =\left|\int_a^b f(y) d y\right| \end{aligned}$ when we were integrating with respect to $\mathrm{x}$, portion below $\mathrm{x}$-axis gave negative area, $\&$ here left part will give negative area. 

 

3. Here Area $=$ Area (I) + Area (II) + Area (III) $=\int_a^b f(y) d y-\int_b^c f(y) d y+\int_c^d f(y) d y$
